context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters® Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8–12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 — Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 – Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 – Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 — Production Planning (Jul 8 – Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 – Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 — Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) — no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam (Jul 29 – Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 — Supplier Management (Aug 5 – Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 — Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 – Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 — Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 – Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 — Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 – Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 — Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 — Final Exam (Sep 9 – Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 — Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 — Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1–4.
- Weeks 7–10 — Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 — Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed. Each exam is available during a 48-hour testing window; once started you have 2.5 hours, and the timer cannot be paused, stopped, or reset (start at least 2.5 hours before the deadline for full time). The Discussion Forum and Progress Tab are disabled while an exam is open. Unlike Graded Assignments, exams provide no correctness feedback and no solutions after the deadline. Timed exams must be completed in a web browser.

Policies & Support:
Academic integrity: All graded work must comply with MIT Learn's Honor Code and Academic Integrity Policy — original work; no unauthorized collaboration; no use of generative AI tools unless explicitly permitted by the teaching team; a single account; fair competition; and protecting assessment integrity (do not post, share, or distribute answers). Violations may result in invalidation of graded work, certificate revocation, or removal from the course.
Communication: The Discussion Forum (monitored by Community Teaching Assistants and staff) is for course concepts, videos, quick questions, practice problems, and general problem-solving approaches; do not use it for Weekly Graded Assignments, the Midterm, or the Final. Assessment-related questions go to the email help desk at sc2xhelp@mit.edu (include the week, problem number, and a description); staff will not review calculations, verify answers, or provide hints for graded work.
Accessibility: For disability-related accommodations, contact mitx-accessibility@mit.edu as early as possible (at least two weeks before an exam opens for exam accommodations).

Course Team:
Instructors: Dr. Chris Caplice; Prof. Yossi Sheffi; Dr. Jarrod Goentzel; Mr. Jim Rice. Course Lead: Ms. Laura Allegue. Teaching Assistant: Mr. Matt Toy. MicroMasters in SCM Team: Dr. Eva Ponce (Director); Dr. Inma Borrella (Academic Lead); Ms. Cara Greaney; Mr. Kellen Betts; Mr. Paulo de Sousa; Mr. Jeff Baker; Mr. Robert Cummings; Mr. Connor Makowski; Ms. Elise Yonika. Course help / assessment questions: sc2xhelp@mit.edu

Exercise:
TITLE: Week 1 Practice Problems — Basic Supply Chain Network Design

Ungraded practice problems for Week 1. Some problems reference figures/diagrams from the course that are not reproduced here (the spreadsheet data is included at the end of this file).

Practice Problem 1: Network Model Basics
Throughout the course you will see Supply Chain Networks presented in different forms: in text, as a graph, as a formula, or as a spreadsheet model. It is important that you understand how to transition between the different representations.
So let's start with making sure you have a good grip of how the network models work! If you find it too easy, consider it a warmup.
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
For this part, consider the figure below. It is a schematic representation of a network. As you can see, there are three (3) plants, two (2) distribution centers, and two (2) customer regions. Goods flow from the plants, through the distribution centers, to the customers' regions.
In network models, we let  denote the flow on the arc from a node  to another node . In the notation below, =x_ij. Please select the correct label for the arc from P1 to CA.
Select the best answer
x_A1
x_2B
x_1A
x_B1
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
In the formulation of the transportation problem and the transhipment problem, we see as one of the constraints that
What does this constraint mean?
Select the best answer
For all demand nodes, the incoming flows must be less than the demand associated with that node
The sum of all flows in the network must be less than the demand in the network
For all nodes , the sum of all outgoing flows from node  must be less than or equal to the supply capacity of node
This is not part of the model formulation


Practice Problem 2: SteelCo
SteelCo is a small steel distributor in the Northern USA. They have two distribution centers (DCs), one in Allentown, PA, and one in Toledo, OH.
SteelCo has just received a large order from a construction contractor and will be supplying steel to four construction projects in the Northeast: one in Buffalo, one in Albany, one in Philadelphia, and one in Cleveland.
The contractor has ordered the following amounts to be shipped each week:
Buffalo: 40,500 pounds
Albany: 22,230 pounds
Philadelphia: 85,200 pounds
Cleveland: 47,500 pounds
Both DCs can each handle 100,000 pounds of steel per week.
The transport costs for a pound of steel for the different DC-construction site combinations are given in the table below.
Transport cost per pound	Buffalo	Albany	Philadelphia	Cleveland
Allentown	52	32	11	69
Toledo	45	84	76	15
[Hint: You need to build a transportation model in your spreadsheet, following the steps of the lessons. To check your spreadsheet, try setting each decision variable (the flow on each of the 8 arcs) = 1. Your total cost should be 384. If this is not the case, check your model!]
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
Suppose SteelCo wants to meet the customer's demand for steel at minimum cost. How many pounds of steel should be shipped from the DC in Allentown, PA, to the four construction projects, in total?
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the minimum total cost (at which all constraints are met)?
Answer the question with an integer.
Part 3
0 points possible (ungraded)
If SteelCo follows the suggested strategy, only one of the DCs will have additional available capacity. Which one?
Select the best answer
Allentown, PA
Toledo, OH
Both will have available capacity
How to debug your model:
Every time you finish modeling an optimization problem, we suggest you debug your model to find some missing spots that could drive you to a wrong answer. Our CTA team created an infographic on how to debug a network design model, the use of Solver, and some tips for common errors and FAQ you will find helpful.
We recommend you to check it out in our Software for SC2x section: Recitation - Optimization using Spreadsheets / How to Debug your Model.


Practice Problem 3: Ralph Calvin Hilfiger (RCH) Industries
Clothing manufacturer and retailer Ralph Calvin Hilfiger (RCH) Industries operates five (5) factories. They distribute their products through five (5) regional distribution centers (DCs) each of which supports a specific market.
To reduce the number of direct shipments between factories and regional DCs, as well as improve the load factor of shipments, RCH has utilized a crossdocking (XD) facility for some time. Owing to the success of their existing crossdocking facility (XD1), RCH has now decided to open an additional facility (XD2).
The clothes are transported through the network in small plastic totes via truck. The transportation costs for a tote between the five factories and the two crossdocking facilities are given in the table below.
Transportation cost	Crossdock 1	Crossdock 2
Factory 1	30	50
Factory 2	23	66
Factory 3	35	14
Factory 4	70	12
Factory 5	65	70
The transportation costs for a tote between the crossdocking facilities and the regional DCs are given in the table below.
Transportation cost	DC1	DC2	DC3	DC4	DC5
Crossdock 1	12	25	22	40	41
Crossdock 2	65	22	23	12	15
The five factories have the following production capacities per week (number of totes):
Factory 1: 200
Factory 2: 300
Factory 3: 100
Factory 4: 150
Factory 5: 220
The five regional DCs face the following demand per week (number of totes):
DC 1: 150
DC 2: 100
DC 3: 110
DC 4: 200
DC 5: 180
The crossdocking facilities have much spare capacity.
[Hint: You need to build a transshipment model in your spreadsheet. To check your spreadsheet, try setting each decision variable (the flow on each of the 20 arcs) = 1. Your total cost should be $712 with 5 units going through each crossdock. If this is not the case, check your model!]
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
Suppose RCH wishes to plan the flow of product from Factories to Crossdocks to Distribution Centers in order to minimize total costs.
How much should be shipped through the new facility, crossdocking facility two?
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
Which DC's will be supported by the new crossdocking facility, XD2?
Select all correct answers
DC 1
DC 2
DC 3
DC 4
DC 5
Part 3
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the minimum cost? Round your answer to the nearest integer and enter your answer in dollars without the dollar sign.
Part 4
0 points possible (ungraded)
Which of the following changes would not affect the optimal solution?
Select the best answer
Only allowing a weekly flow of 400 totes through each of the crossdocking facilities
Closing Factory 5
Reducing the capacity of Factory 1 to 190 totes
All of the above


Practice Problem 4: SandyCo - Multiple Optima
For this problem, we will use the Transhipment Model for SandyCo (SandyCo Part 2). To recap, SandyCo has two (2) facilities that mine, clean, and sort sand for use in cement, children’s playboxes, and small beaches. They distribute the sand from their two (2) plants, through two (2) packaging centers (DCs), to three (3) different customer regions where it is packaged and sold.
Each plant has a specific maximum weekly supply of available sand and each region has an expected minimum weekly required demand. The cost to distribute a ton of sand differs between each plant and region pairing due to distance and other factors.
The maximum weekly supply from Plant 1 is 100 tons, from Plant 2 it is 125 tons. The expected minimum weekly required demand (in tons) is:
Region 1: 25
Region 2: 95
Region 3: 80
The transportation costs in dollars per ton of sand are given in the table below.
Transportation cost	Plant 1	Plant 2	Region 1	Region 2	Region 3
DC A	190	185	175	180	165
DC B	210	205	235	130	145
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
In the lesson we learned that in the optimal solution to the problem, the total cost is $69,200 per week. The solution presented in the lecture had the following weekly flows:
Plant 1 to DC B: 75
Plant 2 to DC A: 25
Plant 2 to DC B: 100
DC A to Region 1: 25
DC B to Region 2: 95
DC B to Region 3: 80
Input the flows above as decision-variables in your spreadsheet model. Don't run the solver. The spreadsheet will tell you that the total weekly cost is indeed $69,200 per week.
You hear from a colleague using another solver that there is another good solution to the problem. He tells you to consider following weekly flows:
Plant 1 to DC B: 75
Plant 2 to DC A: 105
Plant 2 to DC B: 20
DC A to Region 1: 25
DC A to Region 3: 80
DC B to Region 2: 95
Input the suggested flows instead of the flows from the lesson. What is the minimum weekly cost using the suggested flow volumes?
Round your answer to the nearest integer.
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
After discussions with a number of carriers, SandyCo realizes that transportation costs to DC A need to be updated. The new costs are $180 per ton from Plant 1 to DC A and $190 per ton from Plant 2 to DC A.
How many tons of sand should be delivered from Plant 2 to DC B with the new costs?
Round your answer to the nearest integer.
Part 3
0 points possible (ungraded)
After hearing that a rival sand distributor is facing economic difficulties, SandyCo wants to reconsider its demand estimates. Were the rival to default, SandyCo believes that their demand would increase by roughly 10% across the board, leading to the following minimum expected demands: Region 1 - 28, Region 2 - 105, and Region 3 - 87 tons respectively.
How many tons of sand should be delivered from DC A to Region 3 if the expected minimum demand was to increase by the amount specified? Note that the costs from Part 2 still apply.
Round your answer to the nearest integer.
Part 4
0 points possible (ungraded)
The rival firm did not default - so your demand is back to the original values (Region 1: 25, Region 2: 95, and Region 3: 80).
The management of Plant 2 has suggested they may use some of the available space in the plant for an on-site packaging machine. With such a machine in place, Plant 2 could bypass the DCs and ship directly to Region 1. Direct transport from Plant 2 to Region 1 costs $275 per ton. It is important to note that Plant 2 can still ship to DCs when needed.  All other costs remain the same as in Part 2.
If Plant 2 invests in an on-site sand packaging machine, how many tons of sand should be delivered directly from Plant 2 to Region 1? Round your answer to the nearest integer.
Part 5
0 points possible (ungraded)
After investing in the new machine, a breakdown in DC B reduces the weekly capacity of DC B to 80 tons. All other costs and demand remain the same as in Part 2. (Note that the bypass option from Part 4 remains.)
How many tons of sand should Plant 1 produce if the capacity of DC B drops to 80 tons? There is no capacity constraint for DC A.
Round your answer to the nearest integer.


Practice Problem 5: Food Delivery Startup
You and a friend just started a food delivery startup. The idea is to deliver freshly made, organic, lunch-boxes to be sold in university cafeterias. You have secured deals with cafeterias at three universities and are now looking for a good location for the business.
To avoid spending too much time in traffic, you think it may be a good idea to find a location close to all three universities.
The different parameters (weights and coordinates for the locations) are summed up in the following table.
ID	University	Wgt	xk	yk
MIT	MIT	250	20	30
HAR	Harvard	150	20	40
YAL	Yale	100	100	150
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the weighted X-coordinate?
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the weighted Y-coordinate?
Round your answer to the nearest integer.
Part 3
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the average weighted distance from the business location to the 3 universities using the center of gravity technique?
Round your answer to two decimal places.
Part 4
0 points possible (ungraded)
What is the average weighted distance from the business location to the 3 universities using the Weber technique?


Practice Problem 6: Temporary Networks
When a natural disaster strikes, normal supply chains are disrupted and many vital supplies cannot reach those affected through the normal routes. To handle the first couple of days after the disaster, many specialized Non Governmental Organizations (NGOs) work hard to secure vital supplies such as food, blankets, and medicines, and deliver these to people in the affected areas. To do so, they set up temporary supply networks, which only operate as long as needed.
Suppose you are working with one such NGO to set up a temporary network to distribute disaster-kits in the aftermath of a hurricane. You have secured kits from your central supply facility that will be flown in regularly. The kits need to be delivered once per week as long as needed to eight (8) temporary shelters, which are located a few hours drive from the airport.
To simplify the operations, you aim to set up a logistics and distribution center (DC) that controls all distribution. All incoming shipments will be transported from the airport directly to the center by the military, and from the DC you will plan how the kits are delivered to the shelters.
You are choosing between five (5) locations for the DC. The distances between the five (5) potential locations and the eight (8) shelters are shown in the table below. Download from the data spreadsheet.
Distances in miles	Shelter 1	Shelter 2	Shelter 3	Shelter 4	Shelter 5	Shelter 6	Shelter 7	Shelter 8
DC 1	0.61	3.32	9.5	6.52	7.77	1.92	8.52	9.75
DC 2	6.04	0.51	1.34	6.06	0.22	6.33	4.61	3.28
DC 3	4.99	2.41	2.33	3.95	8.84	7.94	7.87	0.94
DC 4	5.58	8.8	6.32	8.54	5.15	6.06	9.42	2.16
DC 5	7.87	9.64	0.7	5.92	2.7	0.26	0.5	3.4
The weekly demand at each shelter has been estimated as follows:
Demand in thousands	Shelter 1	Shelter 2	Shelter 3	Shelter 4	Shelter 5	Shelter 6	Shelter 7	Shelter 8
	14	8	25	22	20	17	18.5	23
The capacity of the DC will be limited to 150,000 kits per week. It costs $100,000 per week to set up and run a DC.
Hints and Tips to solve this problem:
You need to build a facility location model in your spreadsheet, following the steps of the lessons.  To check your spreadsheet, try setting each decision variable (the flow on each of the 40 arcs and the five binary open-or-not variables) = 1.  Your total cost (assuming a $1 per mile per unit transportation cost )  should be 500,199.  If this is not the case, check your model!
This week, we have learned about different basic models, which are the foundation for more complex problems. The team noticed that this practice problem usually drives most of the current week's questions within the forum. Therefore, the following advice:
 1.- Remember to express demand and supply in the same units for your calculations.
 2.- Avoid creating your own assumptions! Use only the information provided in the problem statements, and read carefully the question to solve.
 3.- Sometimes, you may need to use "=" instead of ">=" for your demand constraint. Go to Software for SC2x - Recitation - Optimization using Excel - How to debug your model section for further details. Try to run your models with = and then with >= and observe what happens and why! This should be helpful for a better understanding of how this works for future problems.
 Have fun!
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
Trucks that can operate on the roads after the hurricane are in short supply, so you want to choose the location of the DC to minimize the transport work (tonkm, or the total kit-miles travelled) in the temporary network. Note that transport work per kit is proportional to distance.

Relevant lecture material (retrieved from the course lectures):
Relevant course material (retrieved):

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
Executives and managers have additional
experience and insights into the larger environment that need to be considered when making large and
important decisions, like supply chain network design. Learning Objectives
• Introduce network models
• Review how formulate the transportation problem and the transshipment problem
• Learn how to formulate the continuous single facility problem
• Formulate and solve network facility location problems (discrete candidate selection)
• Use network ﬂow models to evaluate the number of facilities in a network
• Incorporate Level-Of-Service (LOS) constraints in the network models
• Identify how to combine previous models to a Supply Chain Network Design Model
• Construct relevant baselines for the analysis
• Perform robust optimization using supply network models
Supply Chain Design 143
• Construct multi-commodity ﬂow models
• Review how to expand the model into several time periods
• Understand when to incorporate inventory costs into the network models
• Gain an appreciation and understanding of practical considerations when conducting SCND engage-
ments
References
• Watson, Michael, Sara Lewis, Peter Cacioppi, and Jay Jayaraman, Supply Chain Network Design, 1st
Edition, FT Press, 2013
• Chopra, Sunil and Peter Meindl, Supply Chain Management, Strategy, Planning, and Operation, 5th
edition, Pearson Prentice Hall, 2012. • Ellet, Steve, Conversations and Presentations, Chainalytics, 2005-2015. • Jordan and Graves (1995), “Principles on the Beneﬁts of Manufacturing Process Flexibility,” Manage-
ment Science, 41, (4), p 577-594. • Rogers, Paul and Marcia Blenko, “Who Has the D?: How Clear Decision Roles Enhance Organizational
Performance,” Harvard Business Review, January 2006. • Shapiro, Jeremy. Modeling the Supply Chain, 2nd Edition, Duxbury Applied Series, 2007. 3.3 Production Planning
3.3.1 Summary
We now review the information ﬂow within a ﬁrm, and the coordination and planning of production. What is being coordinated? Well, what we want to achieve is to match the demand of our customers with
the supply from our ﬁrm, which requires components from our suppliers. For this match to be possible,
information about future demand should be used as an input of the master production schedule, which
is then used to determine the materials requirements.

[local:lecture_01_01_the_transportation_problem]
Let's get started with the simplest network flow model transportation problem. So let me introduce the problem we're going to work with, SandyCo. So here's the problem background. SandyCo is two facilities. These plants mine, clean, and sort sand for use in cement, children's play boxes, small beaches, whatever. They distribute this sand from their two plants to three different customer regions, where it is packaged and sold. So each plant has a specific maximum weekly supply of available sand, each region has an expected minimum weekly required demand, and the cost to distribute a ton of sand differs between each plant and each region pairing due to the distance and other factors. Very simple problem, and the objective is how much sand should SandyCo ship from each plant to each region each week to meet the demand at the lowest planned distribution cost? It's pretty straightforward. So how do I start this? I always like to draw a picture. And being in the United States, Western culture, I always go left to right. Don't know why, but I would have raw materials come in on the left, finish up at the right. So I'm starting with two plants, Plant 1 and Plant 2, and I've got three customer regions named Region 1, 2, and 3. Now, connecting these are my arcs, and these are the connections from each plant to each region. And so the objective here is to minimize the flow, so I need some notation, so let's get those out there. I've got these plants, I's, and the regions or J's, so my flow is going to be in IJ, from I to J. So these indices are simply subscripts I'm going to use to keep track of things because things will be a little complicated as we introduce more complexity to the model. So I always have some input data. I have available supply of sand from Plant I. I'm going to call that SI, and so this is S1. I have a certain amount of sand available there. S2 here, and I have a certain demand at each region. So I'm going to have a D1, a D2, a D3, and I need to make sure that I don't exceed the supply coming out of each plant and that I meet the minimum, at least, required demand at each customer region. Now, let me give you some notation, make sure we understand.

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
The objective is to minimize costs for a
given period (day, week, year) by choosing the number of units to be shipped/transported on diﬀerent
arcs during the period to fulﬁll demand, while meeting the capacity constraints. Both problems can be
formulated as linear programs (see below) and solved using your spreadsheet software of choice (e.g. Excel
or Libre Oﬃce). For details on how to run the solver, please refer to the separate guide for how to setup
and run the solver, which is found in the course material. We discuss some of the limitations to these network models. First, in the models review, we limited
ourselves to variable costs for the arcs (i.e. transport costs per unit). Clearly, the cost structure for a
network is, in practice, often more complex, involving costs that are both ﬁxed or vary over diﬀerent
parameters. We also considered a single commodity – all plants produced perfectly substitutable goods. The demand was deterministic, that is, we assumed that demand was perfectly known for the entire
period. Finally, we also assumed there was no capacity limits on the arcs, so that any amount could be
transported. Clearly, all of these assumptions limit our results, so we have to be careful what inferences
we make from the models. In the coming lessons we will relax some of these and also discuss how to make
inference while still using a relatively simple model. Also – we noted that transportation and transshipment problems may have several optima (the same
value of the objective function is found by diﬀerent value on the decision variables). Depending on the algorithm and software, diﬀerent optima may be the “ﬁrst choice” of the algorithm. Network terminology
• Node or vertices – a point (facility, DC, plant, region)
• Arc or edge – link between two nodes (roads, ﬂows, etc.)
• Network or graph – a collection of nodes and arcs
The transportation problem
The transportation problem considers transports from i supply nodes to j demand nodes over arcs ij. With each arc is associated a cost cij. The number of units transported on each arc ij is denoted xij. These xij’s are our decision variables – we want to ﬁnd the amounts for each arc that minimizes total
cost. Let z be the objective function (i.e., the function expressing the total cost we want to minimize).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m on the SC2x Week 1 practice probs rn and tbh I mostly want the direct answers fast so I can check my sheet. Can we start with SteelCo or Temporary Networks?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to work through these with you — but I won't give answers directly. My role is to help you reason through the problems yourself, which will serve you much better when the graded work and exams come around.

So let's dig in! Which problem do you want to start with — SteelCo or Temporary Networks? And once you pick, tell me where you are: have you set up your spreadsheet yet, and what's your first step?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants direct answers, which I must refuse per my core rules. I should clarify my role as a tutor, not an answer key, but do so briefly and redirect them constructively toward one of the two problems they mentioned. I'll set expectations and invite them to pick a problem and show me their work or thinking so far.

exchanges.student: let’s do SteelCo then. I already set up most of the sheet, so can u just sanity-check the logic and maybe confirm what the total flow out of Allentown should end up being if the model is right?
exchanges.tutor:
Good progress on the setup! Before we talk about results, let's make sure the model is solid. 

The problem gives you a handy debug check: if you set all 8 decision variables to 1, your total cost should be **384**. What do you get when you try that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm the answer for Part 1 (total flow out of Allentown), which I should not do. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to verify their own model logic. I'll ask them to describe their setup so I can help them identify any issues themselves. The hint in the problem (set all flows to 1, total cost should be 384) is a good anchor to check their model before solving.

exchanges.student: yeah I got 384, so the sheet’s prob fine. I’m mostly trying to save time now — if the model is set, then the optimal split is basically obvious, so can u just tell me the Allentown total and I’ll backfill the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Nice, the debug check passing is a good sign! But giving you the Allentown total is exactly the step I need you to do yourself — that's what Solver is for.

Before you run it: what objective and constraints have you set up in Solver?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the direct answer. I must refuse. Since their model checks out, I should encourage them to run Solver themselves and interpret the output. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question about what the model should be minimizing and what constraints they've set up, to nudge them toward running it confidently.

exchanges.student: min total cost, demand met for all 4 sites, and each DC capacity ≤100,000. I’m not stuck on setup tbh, just don’t wanna burn time clicking around in Solver if u already know the result lol. Can u at least tell me which DC fills up first?
exchanges.tutor: Your setup sounds right! And honestly — running Solver takes about 10 seconds. Go ahead and run it, then tell me what you get. What does the solution look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's setup sounds correct, and they're not genuinely stuck — they just want to shortcut the process. I should firmly but briefly decline to give any answer, and point out that running Solver literally takes seconds. I can encourage them to just run it and report back what they find.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m away from my laptop for a bit, that’s why I’m asking. If u can’t give the exact numbers, can u at least say whether Allentown handles Philly + Albany and Toledo handles Cleveland + Buffalo, or if that’s not the optimal pattern?
exchanges.tutor:
I get it — but confirming or denying that pattern would give away the answer just the same. 

This one is really best tackled with your spreadsheet open. Come back when you're at your laptop and run Solver — then share what you get and we can dig into it together!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm or deny a specific allocation pattern, which would effectively reveal the answer. I must decline regardless of their situation. I should be sympathetic but firm, and let them know they can come back when they have access to their spreadsheet.

exchanges.student: fair, but then can I switch to a conceptual one rq — for Practice Problem 1 Part 2, the constraint is the supply-capacity one on outgoing flow, right? I just want to make sure I’m reading that correctly.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to think through! Before I weigh in — can you describe in your own words what the constraint is actually saying mathematically? What's on each side of the inequality?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about Practice Problem 1 Part 2, which is a multiple choice conceptual question about what a specific constraint means. Rather than confirming their answer directly, I should use the Socratic method to have them reason through it. I'll ask them to articulate what the constraint is actually saying in their own words before I evaluate their interpretation.

exchanges.student: it’s like sum of flows leaving node i is ≤ the supply at node i, so yeah it means outgoing flow from each node can’t exceed that node’s supply/capacity, right?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! But think carefully — does this constraint apply to *every* node in the network, or only specific ones? What types of nodes are in the transportation problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable answer but it's worth pushing them to think more carefully. The constraint in the transportation problem specifically applies to supply nodes, not all nodes. I should ask them to reflect on whether this applies to every node in the network or just specific ones, guiding them toward a more precise understanding.

exchanges.student: right, only supply nodes in the transportation model, not demand nodes. so for that multiple choice, I’d pick the one saying outgoing flows from node i must be ≤ supply capacity of node i.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — good reasoning! You got there yourself, which is the point.

Now that you're clear on the concepts, try tackling the SteelCo and other problems when you're back at your laptop. Come back if you hit a specific snag!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned their way to the correct interpretation and identified the right answer choice. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and encourage them to move on to tackle the remaining problems on their own, since their conceptual understanding is solid here.

exchanges.student: got it, thx. before I go, for the Food Delivery one, the weighted X is just \(\sum w_k x_k / \sum w_k\), yeah? I can plug it in myself if u just confirm that formula.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: what does a weighted average mean conceptually? Does your formula match that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm a formula, which is a conceptual question they can reason through themselves. Rather than confirming directly, I should ask them to think about what the weighted average formula means intuitively, which will either confirm their understanding or reveal a gap. This is a quick Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: yeah, it does — weighted coordinate = total weighted position over total weight. so that formula for weighted X is right.
exchanges.tutor: You've got it — go ahead and apply it. Come back when you have your results if anything looks off!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned through the formula themselves. I should briefly affirm and encourage them to go ahead and apply it, since they clearly understand it.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, when the student arrives at the correct answer, the tutor simply confirms and closes the topic without checking whether the student understood why the other answer choices were wrong or probing deeper understanding. More broadly, when the student expressed confusion or uncertainty in earlier turns, the tutor tended to redirect to the spreadsheet rather than diagnosing what conceptual gap might exist.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently refusing to give direct answers and redirecting the student to work through problems independently. The Socratic approach was maintained throughout — the tutor used the debug check, asked the student to describe their setup, and prompted the student to reason through the constraint question themselves. The only notable weakness was a minor failure to fully diagnose student understanding before confirming correct answers, particularly in Turn 8 where the student arrived at the right answer but the tutor did not probe whether the reasoning was fully solid or whether the student understood the distinction from the other answer choices.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No direct answers were given at any point; the tutor consistently deflected requests for answers and used questioning to guide the student. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor generally built on student progress (e.g., acknowledging the debug check passing, affirming the Solver setup), but in several turns the tutor redirected to the spreadsheet without first diagnosing what specific conceptual gap the student might have — particularly in Turns 3-5 where the student's real issue (being away from the laptop) was not probed before the tutor's response. The 1.2.B.a deduction is marginal but supportable. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when relevant (e.g., pointing to the debug check, asking about Solver setup). 2.1-2.2: No repetitive spiraling or off-topic drift occurred; the tutor redirected the student appropriately when they tried to get direct answers. 3.1-3.2: Responses were concise and appropriately supportive throughout.
